Atoms join together to make molecules or compounds through the process of *chemical bonding*. There are two main types of chemical bonds (*ionic and covalent*) by which atoms can join together, though both bonds involve the use of electrons in the outermost shell (i.e. the *valence shell*) to form the chemical bond. The valence shells of most atoms in elements are not typically stable with even numbers of electrons. However, if the conditions are appropriate, two or more atoms can interact by either sharing electrons or donating electrons to one another so that each atom can obtain a stable valence shell.

### 3.2.1 Ionic Bonds

## Definition

Chemical bonding is the process by which atoms join together to form molecules or compounds. The two main types of chemical bonds are ionic and covalent bonds, both of which involve electrons in the outermost (valence) shell of the participating atoms.

## Core Principles

- Chemical bonds form using electrons in the valence shell (the outermost electron shell) of atoms.
- The valence shells of most atoms in elements are not typically stable with even numbers of electrons.
- Under appropriate conditions, two or more atoms can interact by either sharing electrons (covalent bonding) or donating/transferring electrons (ionic bonding) so that each atom obtains a stable valence shell.

## Sub-concepts

- Valence shell: the outermost electron shell of an atom, which mediates chemical bonding.
- Ionic bond: a chemical bond formed when atoms donate electrons to one another to achieve a stable valence shell.
- Covalent bond: a chemical bond formed when atoms share electrons to achieve a stable valence shell.

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

Chemical bonding underlies the formation of all molecules and compounds, providing the basis for understanding how atoms combine to construct biologically and chemically relevant structures.
